questions.question: Which capability is **native to Secrets Manager but NOT to Parameter Store**?
options: Scheduled, managed credential rotation via a Lambda function
Storing values encrypted at rest with KMS
Retaining a version history of past values
Storing values under hierarchical, slash-delimited paths
questions.explanation: Managed scheduled rotation (via a Lambda) is the headline differentiator of Secrets Manager; Parameter Store has no native rotation and you bolt it on with EventBridge + Lambda. KMS encryption (SecureString), version history, and hierarchical paths are all available in Parameter Store.

questions.question: A team wants to store 40,000 plain, non-rotating configuration values as cheaply as possible. What should you recommend?
options: Parameter Store Standard-tier parameters, which are free
Secrets Manager secrets, one per value
Secrets Manager with a single JSON secret holding all 40,000 values
Parameter Store Advanced tier, required for any count above a few thousand
questions.explanation: Parameter Store Standard tier (String/SecureString) is free and built for hierarchical config, so 40,000 plain values cost nothing. Secrets Manager bills per secret per month, making 40,000 secrets an expensive recurring bill. Advanced tier is paid and unnecessary for plain config of this kind.

questions.question: By default, which version of a Secrets Manager secret does `GetSecretValue` return?
options: The version labeled `AWSCURRENT`
The version labeled `AWSPENDING`
The version labeled `AWSPREVIOUS`
The most recently created `VersionId`, regardless of label
questions.explanation: `AWSCURRENT` is the active version returned by default. `AWSPENDING` is the new value staged mid-rotation, and `AWSPREVIOUS` is the prior current value kept for rollback. The default is keyed off the label, not raw creation order.

questions.question: KMS symmetric `Encrypt` can directly encrypt arbitrarily large payloads, so envelope encryption is only an optional performance optimization.
questions.explanation: KMS symmetric `Encrypt` is capped at 4 KB of plaintext. Envelope encryption (via `GenerateDataKey`) is what lets you encrypt gigabytes — it is not merely an optimization but the mechanism required to exceed the 4 KB ceiling.

questions.question: An app role can call `GetSecretValue` but still gets `AccessDeniedException` reading a Secrets Manager secret encrypted with a customer-managed KMS key. What permission is most likely missing?
options: `kms:Decrypt` on the KMS key that encrypts the secret
`secretsmanager:PutSecretValue` on the secret
`kms:GenerateDataKey` on the KMS key
`ssm:GetParameter` on the parameter path
questions.explanation: Reading an SSE-KMS-encrypted secret requires two independent gates: `GetSecretValue` on the secret AND `kms:Decrypt` on the encrypting key. With `GetSecretValue` already present, the missing piece is `kms:Decrypt`. `PutSecretValue` is for writing, and `ssm:GetParameter` is irrelevant to Secrets Manager.

questions.question: Right after a scheduled rotation, an ASP.NET Core service suddenly fails database authentication. The cached secret was last fetched an hour ago. What is the correct fix on the request path?
options: On the auth failure, invalidate the cache, re-fetch `AWSCURRENT`, and retry once
Switch all reads to the `AWSPENDING` label so they always get the newest value
Disable caching and call `GetSecretValue` on every request
Roll the secret back by promoting `AWSPREVIOUS` to `AWSCURRENT`
questions.explanation: The cached value is stale post-rotation; the resilient pattern is to catch the auth failure, invalidate the cache, re-fetch `AWSCURRENT`, and retry once. `AWSPENDING` is the in-progress value (not necessarily live), per-request calls reintroduce cost/throttling, and rolling back fights the rotation rather than fixing the staleness.

questions.question: Why can a principal with an IAM policy granting `kms:Decrypt` still be denied use of a KMS key?
options: The key policy is the root authority and must delegate to IAM (e.g. the `Enable IAM User Permissions` statement) for IAM grants to take effect
IAM policies never apply to KMS keys; only grants can authorize a key
`kms:Decrypt` must be granted in an SCP before any IAM policy is evaluated
The key must have a `kms:ViaService` condition or all IAM access is blocked
questions.explanation: Every KMS key has a key policy that always governs access; IAM identity policies are effective only if the key policy delegates to IAM (typically the `Enable IAM User Permissions` statement allowing the account root). IAM can authorize a key, grants are not the only mechanism, SCPs are not a precondition here, and `kms:ViaService` is an optional scoping condition, not a requirement.

questions.question: A security audit requires that the app role may use a CMK to read SSE-KMS S3 objects but must never call `Decrypt` directly against the key. Which condition achieves this?
options: `kms:ViaService` equal to `s3.<region>.amazonaws.com` on the role's key permissions
`kms:EncryptionContext` requiring an S3 bucket name
`aws:PrincipalOrgID` restricting the role to the org
`kms:GrantIsForAWSResource` set to true
questions.explanation: `kms:ViaService` restricts key use to requests arriving through a named service endpoint, so the role can use the key for S3 SSE-KMS but a direct KMS `Decrypt` call is denied. Encryption context scopes which ciphertexts decrypt but doesn't block direct API calls; the other two conditions don't gate by service.

questions.question: When KMS automatically rotates a customer-managed symmetric key, you must re-encrypt existing data because the old backing material is discarded.
questions.explanation: Automatic rotation generates new backing material (annually by default, configurable) but retains all previous material under the same key ID/ARN. Existing ciphertext still decrypts transparently and no re-encryption is required; new encryptions use the latest material.

questions.question: For an active-active deployment where two Regions must both decrypt the same ciphertext without calling back to a single origin Region, which KMS feature is correct?
options: A multi-Region key (primary plus replicas sharing key material)
Two independent single-Region CMKs with the same alias name
A single-Region CMK with cross-Region `Decrypt` calls to the origin
An AWS managed key, which is automatically global
questions.explanation: Multi-Region keys are a primary plus replicas that share the same key material and key ID suffix, so each Region decrypts locally with no cross-Region call. Same-named aliases over independent keys would NOT share material (ciphertext wouldn't cross-decrypt), single-Region keys force a cross-Region call, and AWS managed keys are single-Region.

questions.question: An ECS task definition uses a `secrets` block to inject a Secrets Manager value into an environment variable. Which role must hold `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` and `kms:Decrypt`?
options: The task execution role, because the ECS agent fetches the value before the container starts
The task role, because the application code reads the secret at runtime
The EC2 instance profile of the underlying container host
The IAM user who registered the task definition
questions.explanation: With injected secrets, the ECS agent (not your app) resolves the value at task launch, so the task execution role needs `GetSecretValue` + `kms:Decrypt`. The task role governs the application's own runtime AWS calls, not the agent's injection step.

questions.question: In the snippet, what does `dk.CiphertextBlob` hold, and how should it be treated?
options: The encrypted data key — persist it alongside the ciphertext; it is decrypted later via `Decrypt`
The plaintext data key — keep it in memory and never write it to disk
The KMS key's exported symmetric material — store it in a vault
The encrypted payload of your data — this is the final ciphertext to store
questions.explanation: `GenerateDataKey` returns both `Plaintext` (use locally, then wipe) and `CiphertextBlob` (the encrypted data key you persist next to your data and later pass to `Decrypt`). It is not the plaintext key, not exported KMS master material (which never leaves KMS), and not your encrypted payload.

questions.question: A data key was generated with `EncryptionContext { ["app"] = "billing" }`. What is required to successfully `Decrypt` the encrypted data key later?
options: Supply the same `EncryptionContext` key-value pair on the `Decrypt` call
Supply the encryption context as an extra secret password the caller must know
Nothing — encryption context is metadata only and not enforced on decrypt
Supply a different encryption context, since it must change per operation
questions.explanation: Encryption context is non-secret additional authenticated data (AAD) bound into the ciphertext; the same context must be supplied on decrypt or the call fails. It is not a secret password, it is enforced (not just metadata), and it must match exactly — not change per operation.

questions.question: Which statements about KMS key types are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: A customer managed key (CMK) lets you author the key policy, enable rotation, and create grants
AWS managed keys (e.g. `aws/secretsmanager`) are visible in your account but you cannot edit their key policy
AWS owned keys are visible in your account and you control their rotation
Customer managed keys can be shared cross-account and audited via CloudTrail
questions.explanation: CMKs give full control over policy, rotation, and grants, and support cross-account use and CloudTrail auditing. AWS managed keys are visible but their policies are not editable by you. AWS owned keys are invisible and fully AWS-controlled, so the third statement is false.

questions.question: When is `GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext` the right call instead of `GenerateDataKey`?
options: When you need only the encrypted data key now and will decrypt it elsewhere/later before using it
When you want to encrypt a payload immediately in the same process
When you must export the KMS master key material for offline use
When the plaintext to encrypt is under 4 KB and fits a direct `Encrypt` call
questions.explanation: `GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext` returns only the encrypted data key, fitting 'encrypt now, decrypt later elsewhere' workflows where you don't need the plaintext key in the current context. To encrypt immediately you want the plaintext form from `GenerateDataKey`; KMS never exports master material; and a sub-4 KB payload is the direct-`Encrypt` case, unrelated to data keys.

questions.question: Why does a least-privilege Secrets Manager policy resource end in `-??????` (e.g. `secret:prod/myapp/db-??????`)?
options: It matches the random 6-character suffix Secrets Manager appends, targeting exactly one secret and avoiding prefix collisions
It is a wildcard that grants access to every secret whose name starts with `prod/myapp/db`
It restricts the policy to six specific version IDs of the secret
It is required syntax for any Secrets Manager ARN and has no security effect
questions.explanation: Secrets Manager appends a random 6-character suffix to each secret's ARN; the six `?` wildcards match exactly that suffix so the policy targets one specific secret rather than colliding with other secrets sharing the name prefix. It is not a broad prefix match, not version IDs, and it does have a real least-privilege effect.

questions.question: Which KMS keys support automatic rotation? (Select all that apply.)
options: A customer-managed symmetric CMK
An AWS managed key (rotated on AWS's schedule)
An asymmetric CMK
A key created with imported key material
questions.explanation: Customer-managed symmetric CMKs support automatic rotation (annual by default), and AWS managed keys rotate automatically on AWS's schedule. Asymmetric keys and imported-material keys do NOT support automatic rotation — they require manual rotation (new key plus alias swap, or re-import).

questions.question: Which scenario most justifies CloudHSM (or a KMS custom key store backed by it) over standard KMS?
options: A contractual mandate for a dedicated, single-tenant FIPS 140-3 Level 3 HSM where you are the sole operator
Encrypting S3 objects at rest with an auditable, customer-managed key
Reducing KMS request costs when encrypting many small objects
Adding cross-Region decryption for an active-active workload
questions.explanation: CloudHSM is the exception chosen for dedication/compliance — a single-tenant FIPS 140-3 Level 3 HSM you solely operate, or a custom crypto stack. Auditable customer-managed encryption is just a CMK in KMS; cost reduction for small objects is S3 Bucket Keys; cross-Region decryption is multi-Region keys — none of those need CloudHSM.
